返回本批次

美国30天内烧掉14年的导弹

US Burned 14 Years of Missiles in 30 Days

trendytechtribe.com 作者 Betelbuddy 分数 55 评论 24 2026/04/03 04:24
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 要点
  2. 烧钱速度不可持续：美国在 30 天内发射了 850 多枚“战斧”巡航导弹，但在 2026 财年预算中只购买了 57 枚。也就是说，一个月消耗了 14.9 年的产量。地面部队是后备部队，而不是计划：随着精确弹药逼近“温彻斯特”（军事俚语，意为“空”的意思），五角大楼正在将第 82 空降兵和海军陆战队部队部署到一个面积是伊拉克四倍、山脉长达 4000 米的国家。负责这个任务的人从未管理过这么大的事情：国防部长皮特·赫格斯 (Pete Hegseth) 的最高指挥权是大约 200 名士兵。他解雇了 110,000 名五角大楼平民，罢免了司法检察长 (JAG) 官员，并宣布“不予宽恕”，这违反了《海牙公约》的教科书规定。军事数学是一个能源数学问题：地面部队每天都会延长霍尔木兹海峡的关闭时间，欧洲天然气储存（已满28.4％，荷兰为6％）距离80％的冬季补充目标进一步耗尽，化肥原料成本飙升，西方食品价格也随之上涨。
  3. 850 战斧。 57 买了。做数学题。在史诗愤怒行动的第 30 天，美国军方已向伊朗发射了超过 850 枚战斧陆地攻击导弹 (TLAM)。每一件的成本高达 360 万美元。仅巡航导弹一项就价值超过 30 亿美元，每两到三天发射一次的速度就超过全年的产量。 2026 财年国防预算资助购买 57 架战斧。将 850 除以 57，结果是 14.9。美国在一个月内就消耗掉了近15年的战斧采购。这不是一个抽象概念。 《华盛顿邮报》的一位消息人士称，中东战区剩余的战斧库存“低得惊人”，接近温彻斯特。更广泛的弹药情况同样黯淡：前 16 天内消耗了 6,000 多枚进攻性和防御性弹药，其中包括约 320 枚精确打击导弹 (PrSM) 和陆军战术导弹系统 (ATACMS)，这几乎占总库存的一半。 CSIS 估计《Epic Fury》的前 100 个小时花费了 37 亿美元。美国企业研究所（AEI）发表了一份直率的评估：“美国看起来无法与实力相当的对手维持长期、高强度的冲突。”阿森纳已经空心 这并不是从伊朗开始的。自冷战结束以来，国防工业基础一直在优化效率，而不是激增能力。 2003年，美国在入侵伊拉克期间发射了超过725枚战斧。罢工频率放缓的部分原因是供应不足。五角大楼在 20 世纪 90 年代末停止购买战斧，生产线也被关闭。 2011年，北约在轰炸利比亚后的一个月内就耗尽了精确炸弹，利比亚是一个军事能力只有伊朗一小部分的弱国。欧洲的军火库被清空。美国的弹药无法弥补短缺，因为它们不适合英国和法国的飞机。模式是一致的：快速开火、耗尽、争夺。但争夺需要数年时间。在针对伊拉克和叙利亚伊斯兰国 (ISIS) 的空袭之后，空军参谋长马克·威尔士三世 (Mark Welsh III) 将军表示，更换任何一天投下的炸弹都需要“两年的时间才能获得拨款……再过一两年才能真正将其上架”。联合直接攻击弹药 (JDAM) 战争储备库存直到 2021 年（五角大楼开始重建五年后）才恢复到可接受的水平。 RTX（原雷神公司）于 2026 年 2 月签署了一项框架协议，将战斧产量扩大到每年 1,000 枚，比每年生产约 60 枚导弹增加了 16 倍。但“框架协议”并不是“卡车上的导弹”。预计全面投产需要三到四年的时间。战争现在正在发生。燃烧率=850枚导弹30天≈28战斧/天\text{燃烧率}=\frac{850\text{导弹}}{30\text{天}}\约28\text{战斧/天}年产量=57枚导弹/年≈0.16战斧/天\text{年产量}=57\text{导弹/年}\约0.16 \text{ 战斧/天}美国消耗战斧的速度比制造战斧的速度快 175 倍。导弹耗尽，尸体入内 3 月 28 日，《华盛顿邮报》报道称，五角大楼正在为“在伊朗进行数周的地面行动”做准备。第 82 空降师的部队已部署到该地区，该部队是专门为快速插入敌方领土而设计的。的黎波里号航空母舰上的 2,500 多名海军陆战队员和水兵已携带航空资产和快速反应地面部队进入战区。地面部队总数估计约为 17,000 人，计划进行有限的行动：袭击哈格岛（伊朗的主要石油出口码头）和霍尔木兹海峡附近的沿海地点。称这些为“有限行动”需要忽视伊朗的实际情况。伊朗面积约 636,000 平方英里，大致相当于阿拉斯加的大小，是伊拉克的四倍，人口是伊拉克的三倍，约有 9000 万人。扎格罗斯山脉在西部边境形成了长达1600公里的屏障，山峰超过4000米。厄尔布尔士山脉横贯北部，达马万德山海拔 5,671 米。它们之间是广阔的沙漠。与伊拉克提供从海湾港口到巴格达的高速通道不同，伊朗的地形不允许轻易的陆路交通。山口形成了咽喉要道，经过数十年准备的防守者很容易就能封锁。一位国防分析家将其比作“从旧金山越过内华达山脉，穿过内华达州到达盐湖城，与了解地形的对手作战”。伊朗军队不是伊拉克被掏空的军队。现役部队人数约为 610,000 人，其中包括 190,000 名伊斯兰革命卫队 (IRGC) 人员。储备和动员潜力超过100万。相比之下，美国入侵了伊拉克，该国面积只有伊朗四分之一，人口只有伊朗三分之一，地势平坦，有 13 万至15万军队与叛乱作战了十年。目前伊朗战区17000名士兵的态势符合离散袭击，而不是持续的地面行动。但当敌人不配合时间表时，离散的袭击有可能变成持续的行动。 《福克斯与朋友》杂志 国防部长 管理此次升级的人是皮特·赫格斯 (Pete Hegseth)，他于 2025 年 1 月 24 日以 51 比 50 的投票结果被确认为国防部长，副总统 JD 万斯 (JD Vance) 打破了平局。三名共和党参议员（柯林斯、穆尔科斯基和麦康奈尔）投票反对他。每个民主党人都反对。赫格斯曾担任明尼苏达陆军国民警卫队步兵军官，曾部署到关塔那摩湾、伊拉克和阿富汗。他获得了两枚铜星勋章和一枚战斗步兵徽章（CIB）。他的最高指挥权是大约 200 名排和连级人员。退伍后，他经营了两家退伍军人非营利组织，但都陷入了财务困境。他还成为福克斯新闻主持人，从 2017 年到 2024 年共同主持《福克斯与朋友周末》。他是现代美国历史上最缺乏经验的国防部长。与他相当的前任包括詹姆斯·马蒂斯（James Mattis，现役 44 年，四星上将，美国中央司令部前司令，负责监督整个中东地区 20 万多名人员）和罗伯特·盖茨（Robert Gates，在中央情报局 (CIA) 工作 26 年，晋升为局长）。参议员塔米·达克沃斯 (Tammy Duckworth) 是一名在伊拉克失去双腿的退伍军人，他称他为“美国历史上经验最缺乏的国防部长”。自上任以来，赫格塞斯做了：
  4. 解雇了 110,000 名五角大楼平民，比他自己设定的目标多了 80%。一名联邦法官迫使五角大楼领导层承认解雇 5,400 名试用期员工的“绩效问题”理由是捏造的。解除了多个军事部门的联合情报组军官的职务，消除了对战争法的法律监督。宣布对伊朗的敌人“不留情面”，这意味着投降的战斗人员不会被俘虏。国际法专家认为这直接违反了《海牙公约》，并根据 1996 年《战争罪法》构成战争罪。废除了平民伤害减轻小组，并在 2026 年国防战略中省略了对平民保护的提及。信号上泄露的打击时间，包括飞机类型、发射时间和目标窗口。五角大楼监察长的一份报告发现，他“冒着美国军人安全的风险”。
  5. 结果是可见的。伊朗人权倡导组织 (HRANA) 报告称，截至 2026 年 3 月下旬，共有 3,461 人死亡，其中包括 1,551 名平民和 236 名儿童。前 10 天，近 10,000 座民用建筑遭到袭击，其中包括 65 所学校、32 个医疗中心和 13 个红新月会设施。 2 月 28 日，以色列对米纳布一所女子学校发动袭击，造成多达 180 人死亡，其中大部分是学童。人权观察组织（HRW）要求将其作为战争罪进行调查。钢铁侠：一些军事人员确实感到受到过于严格的交战规则（ROE）的限制。赫格斯拥有战斗经验和两枚铜星勋章。地面行动可能确实很有限：袭击哈格岛，而不是向德黑兰进军。但“无季度”并不是股本回报率改革。这是武装冲突法所禁止的行为，它创造了允许暴行的指挥氛围。在中东最易防守的地区管理 17,000 名军队需要机构能力，而赫格斯已系统性地废除了机构能力。账单到达你的加油站 在这里，弹药计算不再是五角大楼的问题，而是成为个人问题。空袭本应是遏制的选择：从防区外范围进行精确打击，地面上没有靴子，霍尔木兹在几周内重新开放。这是高盛的基本预测，即中断六周，布伦特原油价格到年底回落至每桶 80 美元。地面部队完全改变了计算方式。如果美国士兵在伊朗领土上作战，伊朗就有最大的动力尽可能长时间地关闭霍尔木兹海峡。该海峡承载着全球约 20% 的石油和全球五分之一的液化天然气 (LNG) 贸易。持续关闭不会造成暂时的价格飙升。它造成了结构性能源短缺，并在每个下游供应链中加剧。这一级联效应已经开始： 天然气价格：3 月底美国全国平均汽油价格达到每加仑 3.98 美元，比一个月前上涨近 80 美分。加州的价格高于 5 美元。布伦特原油的交易价格高于每桶 113 美元，高于战争开始前的约 70 美元。欧洲储气库：截至 3 月 24 日，欧盟储气库容量仅为 28.4%，远低于五年季节性平均水平。德国为22.3%。法国为22.1%。荷兰是欧洲重要的天然气枢纽，这一比例为 6%。欧盟法律要求成员国在冬季之前达到80%的存储量。这意味着在未来六个月内，在没有本应流经霍尔木兹海峡的卡塔尔液化天然气的情况下，产能将增加 51.6 个百分点。卡塔尔液化天然气中断已经摧毁了卡塔尔 17% 的出口能力，预计修复需要三到五年时间。欧洲替代俄罗斯天然气的备用计划刚刚失败。这一切又回到了 2022 年，只不过替换电源坏了。化肥：天然气占氮肥生产成本高达70%。全球尿素贸易约 30% 来自受霍尔木兹海峡关闭限制的国家。另一个主要氮源俄罗斯三月份暂停了硝酸铵出口。如果危机持续下去，预计 2026 年上半年全球化肥价格将上涨 15% 至 20%。现在正是播种季节。如果化肥成本在 2026 年年中之前保持较高水平，那么产量下降将影响到 2026 年末和 2027 年初的收成。从波斯湾发射管中空的导弹到俄亥俄州的食品杂货账单之间的滞后时间大约是六到九个月。美联储冻结：高盛将美国经济衰退概率上调至30%。安永-帕特农 (EY-Parthenon) 预测，经济严重下滑的可能性为 40%，如果油价触及 150 美元，则该可能性将升至 50% 以上。美联储面临着一个典型的陷阱：通胀飙升（彭博社的追踪数据显示，3 月份通胀率为 3.4%，高于 2 月份的 2.4%），而债券收益率却在下降，这表明华尔街对经济衰退的担忧超过对通胀的担忧。降息将有助于经济增长，但也会加剧价格螺旋式上升。利率抑制了本已疲软的经济。没有什么好的举动。两个兵工厂 美国正在同时耗尽两个战略储备：导弹储备和能源储备。国际能源署 (IEA) 协调 32 个国家释放了 4 亿桶战略石油储备，这是该机构 52 年历史上规模最大的一次。特朗普授权从美国战略石油储备 (SPR) 中开采 1.72 亿桶，目前产能利用率约为 60%。这些都是权宜之计，而不是解决方案。他们买的是几周，而不是几个月。还有第三种耗尽现象正在发生，但媒体几乎没有触及。在波斯湾发射的每一枚“战斧”和“反坦克导弹”都是不再出现在太平洋地区的导弹。 16 天内消耗的 320 枚精确打击导弹大约占总库存的一半。印度-太平洋地区的威慑态势，即阻止中国对台湾采取行动的因素，正在被实时掏空，以助长福克斯新闻主持人正在发起的一场战争。作为如何伊朗赢得了一场正在输掉的战争 详细来说，伊朗不需要赢得任何一场战斗。它需要存活足够长的时间才能使损害变得不可逆转。油井关闭、储存溢出、地质破坏：时间是伊朗的武器。弹药的计算只是给了伊朗更多的时间。地面战争将带来更多。那些本可以通过制度纪律控制升级阶梯的人、任何被赫格斯解雇的高级军官、任何被他杀害的 110,000 名平民、任何可能会说“这违反了海牙公约”的 JAG 律师，都已经不在大楼里了。导弹快用完了。石油快用完了。专业知识首先耗尽。

Original Summary

  1. Key Takeaways
  2. The burn rate is unsustainable: The US fired 850+ Tomahawk cruise missiles in 30 days but purchased only 57 in the FY2026 budget. That is 14.9 years of production consumed in a single month. Ground troops are the fallback, not the plan: With precision munitions approaching “Winchester” (military slang for empty), the Pentagon is deploying 82nd Airborne and Marine units into a country four times the size of Iraq with 4,000-meter mountain ranges. The person running this has never managed anything this big: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s highest command was approximately 200 soldiers. He fired 110,000 Pentagon civilians, removed Judge Advocate General (JAG) officers, and declared “no quarter,” a textbook violation of the Hague Convention. The military math is an energy math problem: Every day ground troops extend the Hormuz closure, European gas storage (28.4% full, Netherlands at 6%) drains further from the 80% winter refill target, fertilizer feedstock costs spike, and Western food prices follow.
  3. 850 Tomahawks. 57 Bought. Do the Math. On Day 30 of Operation Epic Fury, the United States military has fired more than 850 Tomahawk Land Attack Missiles (TLAMs) into Iran. Each one costs up to $3.6 million. That is over $3 billion in cruise missiles alone, launched at a rate that exceeds a full year of production every two to three days. The FY2026 defense budget funded the purchase of 57 Tomahawks. Divide 850 by 57 and the result is 14.9. The US just burned through almost 15 years of Tomahawk procurement in a single month. This is not an abstraction. A Washington Post source described remaining Tomahawk stockpiles in the Middle East theater as “alarmingly low,” approaching Winchester. The broader munitions picture is equally bleak: over 6,000 offensive and defensive munitions were expended in the first 16 days, including approximately 320 Precision Strike Missiles (PrSMs) and Army Tactical Missile Systems (ATACMS), which represent nearly half the combined inventory. CSIS estimated the first 100 hours of Epic Fury cost $3.7 billion. The American Enterprise Institute (AEI) published a blunter assessment: “The United States does not look like it can sustain protracted, high-intensity conflict with a near-peer adversary.” The Arsenal Was Already Hollow This did not start with Iran. The defense industrial base has been optimized for efficiency, not surge capacity, since the end of the Cold War. In 2003, the US fired over 725 Tomahawks during the invasion of Iraq. The frequency of strikes slowed partly because supplies ran low. The Pentagon had stopped buying Tomahawks in the late 1990s and production lines were shut down. In 2011, NATO ran out of precision bombs within a single month of bombing Libya, a weak state with a fraction of Iran’s military capability. European arsenals emptied. US munitions could not backfill the shortage because they did not fit on British and French aircraft. The pattern is consistent: fire fast, run out, scramble. But the scramble takes years. After the air campaign against the Islamic State of Iraq and Syria (ISIS), Air Force Chief of Staff General Mark Welsh III stated that replacing a bomb dropped on any given day takes “two years to get the appropriation… another year or two to actually get it on the shelf.” Joint Direct Attack Munition (JDAM) war reserve stocks did not return to acceptable levels until 2021, five years after the Pentagon began rebuilding. RTX (formerly Raytheon) signed a framework agreement in February 2026 to scale Tomahawk production to 1,000 per year, a 16-fold increase over the roughly 60 missiles produced annually. But “framework agreement” is not “missiles on the truck.” The ramp to full production is estimated at three to four years. The war is happening now. Burn Rate=850 missiles30 days≈28 Tomahawks/day\text{Burn Rate} = \frac{850 \text{ missiles}}{30 \text{ days}} \approx 28 \text{ Tomahawks/day} Annual Production=57 missiles/year≈0.16 Tomahawks/day\text{Annual Production} = 57 \text{ missiles/year} \approx 0.16 \text{ Tomahawks/day}The US is consuming Tomahawks 175 times faster than it builds them. When the Missiles Run Out, the Bodies Go In On March 28, the Washington Post reported the Pentagon is preparing for “weeks of ground operations in Iran.” Elements of the 82nd Airborne Division, a unit designed for rapid insertion into hostile territory, have deployed to the region. Over 2,500 Marines and sailors aboard the USS Tripoli have entered the theater with aviation assets and rapid-response ground units. Total ground force posture is estimated at approximately 17,000 troops, with planning for limited operations: raids on Kharg Island (Iran’s primary oil export terminal) and coastal sites near the Strait of Hormuz. Calling these “limited operations” requires ignoring what Iran actually looks like. Iran covers approximately 636,000 square miles, roughly the size of Alaska, four times larger than Iraq, and three times more populous with approximately 90 million people. The Zagros Mountains form a 1,600-kilometer barrier along the western border, with peaks exceeding 4,000 meters. The Alborz Mountains run across the north, with Mount Damavand reaching 5,671 meters. Between them sit vast deserts. Unlike Iraq, which offered high-speed avenues of approach from Gulf ports to Baghdad, Iran’s topography denies easy overland access. Mountain passes create chokepoints easily blocked by defenders who have been preparing for decades. One defense analyst compared it to “fighting from San Francisco over the Sierra Nevada and across Nevada to Salt Lake City against an opponent who knows the terrain.” Iran’s military is not Iraq’s hollowed-out army. Active duty forces number approximately 610,000, including 190,000 Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) personnel. Reserves and mobilization potential exceed one million. For comparison, the United States invaded Iraq, a country one-quarter Iran’s size with one-third its population and flat terrain, with 130,000 to 150,000 troops and fought an insurgency for a decade. The current posture of 17,000 troops in the Iran theater is consistent with discrete raids, not sustained ground operations. But discrete raids have a way of becoming sustained operations when the enemy does not cooperate with the timeline. The Fox & Friends Secretary of Defense The person managing this escalation is Pete Hegseth, confirmed as Secretary of Defense on January 24, 2025, by a 51-50 vote with Vice President JD Vance breaking the tie. Three Republican senators (Collins, Murkowski, and McConnell) voted against him. Every Democrat opposed. Hegseth served as an infantry officer in the Minnesota Army National Guard, deploying to Guantanamo Bay, Iraq, and Afghanistan. He earned two Bronze Stars and a Combat Infantryman Badge (CIB). His highest command was approximately 200 personnel at the platoon and company level. After the military, he ran two veterans’ nonprofits, both of which went into financial distress, and became a Fox News host, co-hosting Fox & Friends Weekend from 2017 to 2024. He is the least experienced Defense Secretary in modern American history. His predecessor equivalents include James Mattis (44 years active duty, four-star general, former commander of US Central Command overseeing 200,000+ personnel across the Middle East) and Robert Gates (26 years at the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA), rising to Director). Senator Tammy Duckworth, a combat veteran who lost both legs in Iraq, called him “the least experienced Defense Secretary in US history.” Since taking office, Hegseth has:
  4. Fired 110,000 Pentagon civilians, 80% more than his own stated goal. A federal judge forced Pentagon leadership to admit the “performance issues” rationale for firing 5,400 probationary employees was fabricated. Removed JAG officers from multiple military branches, eliminating legal oversight on the laws of war. Declared “no quarter” for enemies in Iran, meaning surrendering combatants would not be taken prisoner. International law experts identified this as a direct violation of the Hague Convention and a war crime under the 1996 War Crimes Act. Abolished civilian harm mitigation teams and omitted references to civilian protection from the 2026 National Defense Strategy. Leaked strike timing on Signal, including aircraft types, launch times, and target windows. A Pentagon Inspector General report found he “risked the safety of US servicemembers.”
  5. The results are visible. Human Rights Advocacy in Iran (HRANA) reports 3,461 total deaths including 1,551 civilians and 236 children as of late March 2026. Nearly 10,000 civilian structures were struck in the first ten days, including 65 schools, 32 medical centers, and 13 Red Crescent facilities. On February 28, an Israeli strike on a girls’ school in Minab killed up to 180 people, mostly schoolchildren. Human Rights Watch (HRW) demanded an investigation as a war crime. The steelman: Some military personnel genuinely felt constrained by overly restrictive rules of engagement (ROE). Hegseth has combat experience and two Bronze Stars. The ground operations may be truly limited: Kharg Island raids, not a march on Tehran. But “no quarter” is not ROE reform. It is a prohibited act under the laws of armed conflict that creates a command climate permitting atrocities. And managing 17,000 troops in the most defensible terrain in the Middle East requires institutional competence that Hegseth has systematically dismantled. The Bill Arrives at Your Gas Station Here is where the munitions math stops being a Pentagon problem and becomes a personal one. The air campaign was supposed to be the contained option: precision strikes from standoff range, no boots on the ground, Hormuz reopened within weeks. That was the Goldman Sachs base case, six weeks of disruption, Brent crude retreating to $80 per barrel by year-end. Ground troops change the calculus entirely. If US soldiers are fighting on Iranian soil, Iran has maximum incentive to keep the Strait of Hormuz closed for as long as physically possible. The strait carries approximately 20% of the world’s oil and one-fifth of global liquefied natural gas (LNG) trade. A sustained closure does not create a temporary price spike. It creates a structural energy deficit that compounds through every downstream supply chain. The cascade is already underway: Gas prices: US national average gasoline hit $3.98 per gallon in late March, up nearly 80 cents from a month earlier. California is above $5. Brent crude is trading above $113 per barrel, up from roughly $70 before the war began. European gas storage: EU gas storage sits at just 28.4% of capacity as of March 24, well below the five-year seasonal average. Germany is at 22.3%. France at 22.1%. The Netherlands, a critical European gas hub, is at 6%. EU law requires member states to reach 80% storage by winter. That means refilling 51.6 percentage points of capacity over the next six months, without the Qatari LNG that was supposed to flow through the Strait of Hormuz. The Qatar LNG disruption already destroyed 17% of Qatar’s export capacity, with repairs estimated at three to five years. Europe’s backup plan for replacing Russian gas just blew up. This is 2022 all over again, except the replacement supply is the thing that broke. Fertilizer: Natural gas accounts for up to 70% of nitrogen fertilizer production costs. Approximately 30% of global urea trade originates from countries constrained by the Hormuz closure. Russia, the other major nitrogen source, suspended ammonium nitrate exports in March. Global fertilizer prices are projected 15 to 20% higher in the first half of 2026 if the crisis persists. Planting season is happening right now. If fertilizer costs stay elevated through mid-2026, the yield reduction hits harvests in late 2026 and early 2027. The lag between the missile that emptied a launch tube in the Persian Gulf and the grocery bill in Ohio is approximately six to nine months. The Fed is frozen: Goldman Sachs raised its US recession probability to 30%. EY-Parthenon puts severe downturn odds at 40%, rising above 50% if oil hits $150. The Federal Reserve faces a classic trap: inflation is jumping (Bloomberg’s tracker shows 3.4% in March, up from 2.4% in February) while bond yields are falling, signaling Wall Street fears recession more than inflation. Rate cuts would help growth but feed the price spiral. Rate holds choke an already weakening economy. There is no good move. The Two Arsenals The US is draining two strategic reserves simultaneously: its missile stockpile and its energy reserves. The International Energy Agency (IEA) coordinated a 400-million-barrel release from strategic petroleum reserves across 32 nations, the largest in the agency’s 52-year history. Trump authorized 172 million barrels from the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), currently at roughly 60% capacity. These are stopgaps, not solutions. They buy weeks, not months. And there is a third depletion happening that the press has barely touched. Every Tomahawk and ATACMS fired in the Persian Gulf is a missile that is no longer in the Pacific. The 320 precision strike missiles expended in 16 days represent roughly half the combined inventory. The Indo-Pacific deterrence posture, the thing that keeps China from making a move on Taiwan, is being hollowed out in real time to feed a war that a Fox News host is running into the mountains. As How Iran Wins a War It’s Losing detailed, Iran does not need to win a single battle. It needs to survive long enough for the damage to become irreversible. The well shut-ins, the storage overflows, the geological destruction: time is Iran’s weapon. The munitions math just gave Iran more time. The ground war will give it even more. And the person who could have managed the escalation ladder with institutional discipline, any of the senior officers Hegseth fired, any of the 110,000 civilians he gutted, any of the JAG lawyers who might have said “this violates the Hague Convention,” is no longer in the building. The missiles are running out. The oil is running out. The expertise ran out first.

评论（默认中文）

jotux 2026/04/03 04:38
更好的文章： https://www.cbsnews.com/news/u-s-tomahawks-iran-war-faster-t...

> 最大产量估计为每年 2,330 枚：根据战略与国际研究中心引用五角大楼预算文件的一份报告，雷神公司的三份合同每份产能为 600 枚，BAE 的一份合同每年生产多达 530 枚导弹。

>然而，根据战略与国际研究中心的数据，美国军方的实际采购率约为每年90架。根据国防部预算文件，海军在 2026 财年仅需求 57 枚导弹。

因此，由于采购率低，生产率也低。

A better article: https://www.cbsnews.com/news/u-s-tomahawks-iran-war-faster-t...

>The maximum rate of production is estimated to be 2,330 per year: Three contracts from Raytheon each have a capacity of 600 and a BAE has a contract to produce up to 530 missiles per year, according to a report from the Center for Strategic and International Studies, which cites Pentagon budget documents.

>However, the actual procurement rate for the U.S. military is about 90 per year, according to the Center for Strategic and International Studies. The Navy requested only 57 missiles for fiscal year 2026, according to Defense Department budget documents.

So the rate of production has been low because the procurement rate has been low.

jotux 2026/04/03 05:31
我重写了这篇文章，现在更好了：

战斧于 1983 年投入使用，到 2026 年他们只生产了 57 架。算一下！

这意味着军队只能拥有（2025-1983）* 57 = 2394 架战斧。

但军方表示，他们的库存大约有 3000 至 4000 把战斧。这是一个阴谋吗？如果他们每年只能赚 57 个，他们怎么可能拥有超过 2394 个？！

提示：请给我写一篇关于美国的文章，一年只能生产 57 个托莫霍克导弹，但看起来他们的摩尔比这个还要多

I rewrote the article, it's even better now:

The tomahawk entered service in 1983, in 2026 they only produced 57. DO THE MATH!

This means the military can only have (2025-1983) * 57 = 2394 Tomahawks.

But the military says they have approximately 3000-4000 tomahawks in inventory. Is it a conspiracy? How could they POSSIBLY have more than 2394 if they can ONLY MAKE 57 PER YEAR?!

prompt: rite me article about US only can make 57 tomohok missels a year but looks lik they have moar than that

pfannkuchen 2026/04/03 05:02
美国是否真的公布了武器生产的真实数据？让我感到怀疑，因为从战略上讲，这是非常愚蠢的*。

*是的，现任政府非常愚蠢，但据我所知，他们没有改变这方面的政策，如果有的话，他们比以前的政府更有可能撒谎，对吗？

Does the US actually publish real numbers about weapons production? Color me skeptical, as strategically that would be very foolish*.

在 HN 中查看
atmavatar 2026/04/03 06:48
我认为像这样真正有趣的国防信息是为海湖庄园的浴室里的阅读材料保留的。
I thought the really juicy national defense information like that was reserved for reading material in Mar A Lago bathrooms.
在 HN 中查看
asdff 2026/04/03 05:09
是的，他们确实这样做。归根结底，预算是公开的，当美国政府想要更多的东西时，他们不会在内部实现。他们呼吁提出更多建议，私营公司（例如通用动力公司或雷神公司）竞标具有非常明确的要求的合同。我确信这对外国情报来说是成熟的信息，但几十年来一直如此。

https://www.war.gov/News/Releases/Release/Article/4026238/fa...

Yeah they do. At the end of the day the budgets are public, and when the US government wants more of something they don't make it in house. They put out a call for proposals for more of something, and private companies (e.g. general dynamics or raytheon) bid for the contract with very specifically defined requirements. I'm sure it is ripe information for foreign intelligence but it has been playing out like this for decades at this point.

https://www.war.gov/News/Releases/Release/Article/4026238/fa...

在 HN 中查看
pfannkuchen 2026/04/03 05:11
> 预算是公开的

哈哈

在过去的几十年里，在事实发生几十年后，发生了很多秘密事情的披露。他们停止这样做了吗？这对我来说似乎真的很天真。

> the budgets are public

LOL

There have been so many disclosures of secret things happening in past decades, decades after the fact. Did they stop doing that? This seems really naive to me.

在 HN 中查看
asdff 2026/04/03 05:17
不过，这只是中央情报局的恶作剧，而不是 155 毫米炮弹生产合同。就像他们必须把这些数字列出来，因为这是一个需要填写的订单，而供应商必须能够履行它......
That is stuff like cia shenanigans not 155mm shell production contracts though. Like they have to put those numbers out because its an order to fill and vendors have to be able to fulfill it...
在 HN 中查看
readthenotes1 2026/04/03 07:34
这听起来是一件非常好的事情。如果我们要对抗真正的敌人，我们显然需要拥有更具弹性的供应链。

它谈到伊朗有多大，好像这很重要。中国或俄罗斯呢？它们相当大，不是吗？

This sounds like a very good thing. We obviously need to have a more resilient supply chain if we're going to take on an actual enemy.

It talked about how big Iran is as if that mattered. What about China or Russia? They're pretty big, aren't they?

在 HN 中查看
OutOfHere 2026/04/03 08:08
弹性是好的，但发展增援能力并不是免费的，美国也负担不起。过度的军费开支使苏联破产。

美国没有机会成功地与中国进行直接战斗。几乎可以肯定，中国在美国本土部署了秘密无人机，一天之内就能摧毁美国国内基地。至于中国自己的导弹，它们的分布非常分散，永远无法被消灭。

Resiliency is good, but developing surge capacity is not free, and the US cannot afford it. Excessive military spending bankrupted the USSR.

The US has no chance of ever successfully engaging China in direct combat. China almost certainly has secret drones spread out in the mainland US that will destroy domestic US bases in a single day. As for China's own missiles, they're so spread out that they can never be neutralized.

ljsprague 2026/04/03 04:36
“烧钱速度不可持续：美国在 30 天内发射了 850 多枚战斧巡航导弹，但在 2026 财年预算中只购买了 57 枚。这相当于单月消耗了 14.9 年的产量。”

作者是否认为美国一年只能制造57枚导弹？

"The burn rate is unsustainable: The US fired 850+ Tomahawk cruise missiles in 30 days but purchased only 57 in the FY2026 budget. That is 14.9 years of production consumed in a single month."

在 HN 中查看
palmotea 2026/04/03 05:00
> 您认为美国有闲置产能可以立即启动吗？

我确信一些非常聪明的 MBA 会通过消除闲置产能或进行削减来增加利润，从而使启动变得更加困难。这就是美国的商业文化：专注于本季度或今年，其他都不重要。

> Do you think the US has idle capacity that can be activated at a moment's notice?

I'm sure some very smart MBA increased profits by eliminating spare capacity or making cuts that would make it much harder to spin up. That's American business culture: focus on this quarter or this year, nothing else matters.

righthand 2026/04/03 04:34

在 HN 中查看
ReptileMan 2026/04/03 04:32
这应该是一个很好的警钟，让我们调查 MIC 的生产力极低的情况。伊朗对于空军和​​后勤来说是一次很好的压力测试，而教训是，以目前的生产率，台湾是站不住脚的。

如果美国库存在与第八级对手的一场小冲突之后就如此枯竭，那么许多过去 20 年来负责采购的人就应该失业。

Which should be a good waking up call to investigate the MIC about their abysmally low productivity. Iran is a good stress test for the airforce and logistics - and the lesson is that Taiwan is indefensible with current production rates.

If US stocks are so depleted after something that is barely a skirmish against 8th tier adversary - a lot of people that have been responsible for procurement in the last 20 years should lose their jobs.

在 HN 中查看
lopsotronic 2026/04/03 06:33
无论美国在可行的时间范围内提高生产速度，台湾都可能是站不住脚的。

中国海军/解放军空军/解放军空军部队在 100 公里范围内所能产生的火力之大简直令人惊叹。即使您将三艘福特级航空母舰停放在最佳任务半径内，并且它们的整个编队可以在接近训练的条件下起飞和降落， 和 他们不需要 DCAP 或电子战覆盖，*并且*如果每一枚炸弹和飞机都拥有完美的任务武器来瞄准正确的目标 - 即使有所有这些 不可能的条件 使满意 。 。 相对于台湾局势，你仍然没有产生足够的武器效果来压制中国一半的火力发电设施。他们不需要一半。

这不会是美国海军的作战情况。不，绝对不是。如果我们特别不幸，我们可能永远无法确定“谁”号航空母舰发生了什么——只是它驶入座间味的电子雾中然后消失了。获救的水手还可以补充一点。

当权者知道这一点，当选的政客也知道这一点（但他们不在乎，因为他们的口袋里经常塞满了中国的利益），但我们仍然有不可阻挡的美国霸主的胸膛。是的，我们确实拥有一支非常庞大的军队——这是真的！ - 但这是世界上最大的经济体花费了大量资源来对抗的军队。二十年来。

我最担心的是，捶胸者确实会直接控制，引发灾难，并做出最糟糕的反应。我对对沉没的主要水面资产做出理智的反应没有信心；这些人在心理上不具备承受羞辱的能力。

我们已经在和伊朗调情了——该死的 伊朗 所有人中——许多应该更了解的人已经开始谈论打破首次使用的禁忌。为了 伊朗。

Taiwan might be indefensible at any rate of production the United States could conceivably spin up within a practical time horizon.

The volume of fire that can be generated inside the 100km line by PLAN/PLAAF/PLAARF forces is nothing less than breathtaking. Even if you parked three Ford class carriers inside optimal mission radius, and their entire complement could take off and land in near-training conditions, and they don't need DCAP or electronic warfare coverage, *AND* if every single bomb and aircraft has a glassy-perfect mission right weapon to right target - EVEN WITH all these impossible conditions satisfied . . you're still not generating enough weapon effect to suppress even half of the PRC fire generation complex vis a vis a Taiwan situation. And they don't need half.

And that is not going to be the operational situation for USN. No, not by a long shot. If we're particularly unlucky, we might not ever know for sure what happened to the USS Whoever - just that it sailed into an electronic fog past Zamami and was gone. Rescued sailors could add little more.

The powers-that-be know this, the elected politicians know this (but don't care because they often have pockets stuffed from Chinese interests), but still we have chest beaters of the unstoppable American juggernaut. Yes, we do have a very big military - it's true! - but it's a military that the largest economy in the world has spent a good deal of its resources working to counter. For twenty years.

My greatest fear is that the chest beaters do assert direct control, court disaster, and have the worst possible reaction. I'm not confident in a sane response to a major surface asset being sunk; these are not people mentally geared to handle humiliation.

We're flirting this line already with Iran - with goddamn Iran of all people - where many People Who Should Know Better have already been flapping their mouths about breaking the taboo on First Use. For Iran.

在 HN 中查看
asdff 2026/04/03 05:15
>过去20年里很多负责采购的人应该失业。

如果有什么不同的话，我想说的是，这意味着采购已经更接近“适当规模”。

>a lot of people that have been responsible for procurement in the last 20 years should lose their jobs.

If anything I would say this means procurement has been closer to "right sized" than not.

在 HN 中查看
